name: Aguzzo
classification_notes: Displaay class it under serif / antique. Loosely related to Alessandro Butti and Aldo Novarese's Augustea (Fonderia Nebiolo, 1951), an engravers-style typeface based on Roman capitals. Notable for triangular punctuation and an enclosed figure 2, surfaced as alternate stylistic sets.
summary: An engravers-style serif by Kasper Pyndt Rasmussen, loosely revived from Butti and Novarese's Augustea, with a steep 16 degree italic.
description: A serif by Kasper Pyndt Rasmussen, first sketched in 2015 and released through Displaay in late 2025. Seven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, fourteen styles total, with a one-axis variable cut on Weight. The uprights pair carved geometry with transitional traits, and the italic leans at 16 degrees with broad-nib flourishes. Triangular punctuation and an enclosed figure 2 from Augustea surface as stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Aguzzo as quite loosely related to Alessandro Butti and Aldo Novarese's Augustea, the 1951 Nebiolo engravers face based on Roman capitals. They credit Kasper Pyndt Rasmussen with the design and Igino Marini with spacing and kerning, and frame the family as a high-legibility face that combines carved geometry with transitional traits.

name: Avantt
classification_notes: Displaay class Avantt under sans / grotesque. Originally drawn as a bespoke face for the Bubenska 1 functionalist building in Prague (Adolf Bens and Josef Kriz, 1927 to 1935), with inspiration from Ladislav Sutnar, Adolf Loos and the Bauhaus.
summary: A geometric grotesque by Martin Vacha drawn from Prague's Bubenska 1 functionalist signage, with titling alternates for narrower caps.
description: A geometric sans by Martin Vacha first sketched in November 2020 and released February 2021. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, sixteen styles total, plus a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Slant. Glyph construction echoes stairway bannisters and railings from the original signage commission. Titling Alternates substitute selected uppercase letters in narrower widths for headline variety.
foundry_blurb: Displaay explain Avantt was initially created as a bespoke typeface for the signage and brand at Bubenska 1, a reconstructed Prague office building designed in functionalist style by Adolf Bens and Josef Kriz between 1927 and 1935. The retail version is by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, and they cite Ladislav Sutnar, Adolf Loos and the Bauhaus as reference points.

name: Azeret
classification_notes: Displaay class Azeret under sans / grotesque. The collection holds three families, Azeret, Azeret SemiMono and Azeret Mono. Drawn from OCR fonts, past and futuristic operating systems, nineties interfaces, with a mono-linear character and high x-height. The Mono is free and on Google Fonts.
summary: A mono-linear sans by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek built from OCR and OS lineage, with proportional, SemiMono and Mono families.
description: A sans-serif system by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek released October 2021. Twenty-four weights span the collection across three families (proportional Azeret, Azeret SemiMono and Azeret Mono), forty-eight styles total, with a three-axis variable cut on Weight, Slant and Mono. Stroke contrast is minimal, x-height is high and ascenders and descenders are short. Stylistic alternates lean into nineties interface aesthetics. The monospaced sub-family is free and on Google Fonts.
foundry_blurb: Displaay say the typeface began as an exploration of OCR fonts, past and futuristic operating systems, various interfaces and the nineties, then settled into a face built to serve in operating systems. It is designed by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek, with the monospaced sub-family released free and on Google Fonts. They flag a Martin Ehrlich micro-site for further exploration.

name: Bagoss Condensed
classification_notes: Displaay class Bagoss under sans / grotesque. Condensed is the narrowest of three width families inside the Bagoss Collection. Carries the same hint serif and high contrast, scaled to a tighter width.
summary: The condensed width of Bagoss by Daniel Quisek, narrowed from the standard with the same hint serif and stroke contrast.
description: The Condensed width of the Bagoss Collection by Daniel Quisek, the narrowest of three width families. Twelve styles run across six weights with matching italics, sharing the family's high contrast and tiny single-edge serif. The Bagoss Collection variable cut covers Weight, Slant and Width axes, so designers can move between Condensed, Standard and Extended in one file.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Bagoss Condensed as the narrowest width inside the Bagoss Collection, sharing the family's high stroke contrast and tiny single-edge serif. The collection is designed by Daniel Quisek with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, and pairs cursive forms unlocked through OpenType with three width subfamilies running from condensed to expanded.

name: Bagoss Extended
classification_notes: Displaay class Bagoss under sans / grotesque. Extended is the widest of three width families inside the Bagoss Collection. Carries the same hint serif and high contrast in a wider proportion.
summary: The extended width of Bagoss by Daniel Quisek, the widest cut of the collection with the same hint serif and contrast.
description: The Extended width of the Bagoss Collection by Daniel Quisek, the widest of three width families. Twelve styles run across six weights with matching italics, sharing the family's high contrast and tiny single-edge serif. Use it for posters, mastheads and identity work where wider proportions help the contrast read at scale.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Bagoss Extended as the widest width inside the Bagoss Collection, sharing the family's high stroke contrast and tiny single-edge serif. The collection is designed by Daniel Quisek with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, and the Extended cut completes the family alongside Standard and Condensed widths.

name: Bagoss Standard
classification_notes: Displaay class Bagoss under sans / grotesque. The Bagoss Collection holds three width families: Condensed, Standard and Extended. Stroke contrast was raised over time and a tiny single-edge serif developed, with cursive forms unlocked via OpenType.
summary: The standard width of Bagoss by Daniel Quisek, a high-contrast sans with a tiny single-edge serif and optional cursive forms.
description: The Standard width of the Bagoss Collection by Daniel Quisek, released February 2022. Twelve styles run across six weights with matching italics, and the wider Bagoss Collection extends into Condensed and Extended widths for a total of thirty-six styles, with a three-axis variable cut on Weight, Slant and Width. Stroke contrast was increased through the design and a tiny single-edge serif developed. Cursive forms are unlocked via OpenType features.
foundry_blurb: Displaay say the original Bagoss sketch was a crude condensed sans-serif with a large x-height and diagonal terminals, and that as the typeface matured it gained higher stroke contrast and a tiny single-edge serif. Designed by Daniel Quisek with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, the family grew into three subfamilies running from condensed to expanded widths, with cursive forms revealed through OpenType.

name: Booton
classification_notes: Displaay class Booton under sans / grotesque. The signature feature is a uni-width construction that holds glyph widths constant across weights and styles, designed for stable button layouts and hover states. High x-height with ink traps and tapered stem connections.
summary: A uni-width sans by Daniel Quisek that keeps glyph widths constant across weights, built for buttons and screen UI.
description: A sans by Daniel Quisek first sketched August 2021 and released September 2024. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, sixteen styles total, plus a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Slant. Glyph widths stay constant across weights so button rows and hover states do not shift. High x-height, ink traps and tapered stem joins support readability on screen, with hard-edge inner corners and open upper terminals giving it a modern feel.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Booton as a sans-serif engineered for the digital age, with a uni-width feature that holds glyph widths constant across weights and styles for stable button spacing and hover effects. Designed by Daniel Quisek with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, the family pairs ink traps and strong stem tapering with hard-edge inner corners and open upper terminals.

name: Concrette
classification_notes: Displaay class Concrette under serif / antique. The collection has three contrast cuts: S, M and XL. Construction principles are adapted from the foundry's Dazzed sans, with exaggerated arches and rhythm cues from Trajan's column.
summary: A three-optical-size serif by Martin Vacha that adapts Dazzed's construction with exaggerated arches and Trajan-influenced rhythm.
description: A serif by Martin Vacha first sketched January 2020 and released February 2024. Twenty-four weights span the collection across three optical sizes, S, M and XL, forty-eight styles total, with a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Contrast. XL carries the most stroke contrast for headlines, S has muted contrast and robust serifs for small text, M sits between for medium scales. Construction principles are adapted from Dazzed.
foundry_blurb: Displaay say Concrette grew out of work on their Dazzed sans, with construction principles adapted to a serif framework, exaggerated arches and rhythmic cues from Trajan's column. Designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, they describe it as the zenith of a serif journey that began with Reckless and ran through Tobias and Teodor, with the trio of XL, M and S optical sizes named after composite materials like concrete.

name: Coxx
classification_notes: Displaay class Coxx under other / round. A modular grotesk built without curves or Beziers. The collection holds two families, Coxx and Coxx GAPS, the GAPS cut keeping decorative gaps inside the strokes. The variable cut controls gap width.
summary: A modular constructivist grotesk by Marek Pistora drawn without curves, with a GAPS variant whose stroke gaps open and close on a variable axis.
description: A modular grotesk by Marek Pistora first sketched in 1998 and released through Displaay in September 2025. Seven weights run from Thin to Heavy across two families, fourteen styles total, with a two-axis variable cut on Weight and GAPS. Built entirely without curves, the design started as a poster face for a 2000 Carl Cox gig and matured into a collection of two cuts, a fully filled Coxx and a Coxx GAPS with decorative gaps that open and close on the variable axis.
foundry_blurb: Displaay welcome you into a world of constructivism and deconstructivism with Coxx, a modular grotesk built entirely without curves, no Beziers needed. Designed by Marek Pistora with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, they trace the design back to a poster for a 2000 Carl Cox gig and describe the GAPS variant as reflecting the laser-lit energy of house and techno party culture.

name: Dazzed
classification_notes: Displaay class Dazzed under sans / grotesque. Narrow proportions, originally drawn as a bespoke face for a cinematic project meant to span comic, grotesque, dramatic, technical, action, crime and sci-fi tones. Cut-off terminals on t, f and r contrast classical letterforms elsewhere.
summary: A narrow grotesque by Martin Vacha drawn for a cinematic project, mixing comic, dramatic and technical letterforms.
description: A narrow sans by Martin Vacha released August 2016. Seven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, fourteen styles total, plus a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Slant. The italic sits at 18 degrees by default with lower angles available via the variable file. Letterforms shift between comic, dramatic and quirky moments in a, e, C, G and technical, cold sci-fi cut-offs in t, f and r. Rounded accent dots sit against squared punctuation for clearer recognition.
foundry_blurb: Displaay say Dazzed started as a bespoke typeface for a cinematic project proposal, designed to comprise the look of every kind of movie. Designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, they describe a typeface where comic, grotesque, dramatic moments meet technical, steel cold, action, crime and sci-fi shapes, with italics by default at 18 degrees and lower angles on the variable axis.

name: Denim
classification_notes: Displaay class Denim under sans / grotesque. The Denim Collection has four families: Denim (narrow), Denim WD (wide), Denim INK (rounded inner corners) and Denim INK WD. Italics run at 11 degrees, framed as the golden number after beer grades. Flattened overshoots seat the typeface neatly on the line.
summary: A grotesque by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek with flattened overshoots, a wider WD cut and an INK family with rounded inner corners.
description: A sans by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek first sketched February 2021 and released June 2021. Twenty-four weights span four families across narrow and wide widths, with INK variants that carry rounded inner corners drawn from silkscreen on denim, forty-eight styles total, plus a three-axis variable cut on Weight, Slant and Width. Italics run at 11 degrees. Flattened overshoots help the type sit cleanly on the line. Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Denim as a sans-serif that fits any situation, great for small text and confident in large headlines. Designed by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, the family pairs flattened overshoots with italics at the golden 11 degree angle, and extends into a wider Denim WD plus an INK family whose rounded inner corners come from silkscreen printing on denim.

name: Documan
classification_notes: Displaay class Documan under other / round. Semi-rounded forms with a typewriter aesthetic, but not monospaced. Circular forms are punched into vertical stems with diagonal rounded terminals. Originally bespoke for the Charta 77 Foundation. Collection: Documan, Documan STC stencil (free) and Documan CNT construct.
summary: A semi-rounded typewriter-flavoured sans by Martin Vacha, with a free stencil sibling and a decorative construct cut.
description: A semi-rounded sans by Martin Vacha first sketched June 2013 and released May 2019. Twenty-seven weights span the Documan Collection across three families, twenty-seven styles total, with a one-axis variable cut on Weight. Circular forms are punched into vertical stems with diagonal rounded terminals, and unexpected serifs surface on D, E and P. Documan STC is a free stencil cut. Documan CNT is a decorative constructed cut where parts go missing even where stencil production would not need them.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Documan as a semi-rounded typeface with a typewriter aesthetic that is not actually monospaced, originally drawn as a bespoke face for the Charta 77 Foundation visual identity. Designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, the collection now includes Documan STC, a free stencil cut, and Documan CNT, a decorative constructed family missing parts even where stencil production would not require it.

name: Fellix
classification_notes: Displaay class Fellix under sans / grotesque. Mono-linear geometric sans in the foundry's #ellix system, alongside Hellix, Gellix and Yellix. Circular forms are connected gracefully onto vertical stems with diagonal terminals. Inspired by Renner's Futura sketches, Drescher Grotesk, Super Grotesk and Johnston.
summary: A geometric mono-linear sans by Martin Vacha in the #ellix family, with circle forms attached gracefully to stems and diagonal terminals.
description: A geometric sans by Martin Vacha first sketched October 2011 and released August 2015. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, sixteen styles total, plus a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Slant. In Fellix, circle forms connect gracefully onto vertical stems and terminals are diagonal, distinguishing it from sibling cuts Hellix, Gellix and Yellix. Stylistic alternates lean into stricter, colder forms.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Fellix as a mono-linear geometrical sans-serif designed by Martin Vacha during a period of falling for Paul Renner's first Futura sketch. They place it inside an #ellix family alongside Hellix, Gellix and Yellix, with circle forms connecting gracefully onto vertical stems and diagonal terminals, and cite Drescher Grotesk, Super Grotesk and Johnston as further references.

name: Fenul
classification_notes: Displaay class Fenul under serif / antique. The Fenul Collection has three width families: Standard, Condensed and Compressed. Construction is inspired by anatomical bones, with thinner centres and heavier ends. Awarded ADC Award 2023 and Visuelt 2023.
summary: A high-contrast serif by Jana Papierova drawn from anatomical bones, with Standard, Condensed and Compressed widths.
description: A serif by Jana Papierova first sketched April 2021 and released October 2023. Twenty-four weights span the collection across three width families, forty-eight styles total, with a three-axis variable cut on Weight, Width and Slant. Glyphs carry thinner centres and heavier ends, echoing the design brief from a chapter on bones. Slanted italics and true italics live in one variable file, with true cursive forms activated through stylistic sets. Awarded ADC Award 2023 and Visuelt 2023.
foundry_blurb: Displaay say Fenul's design is inspired by an anatomy book, specifically the chapter on bones, where the characteristic thinner centres and heavier ends drive the construction. Designed by Jana Papierova with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, the family combines slanted italics with true italics in one variable font and was awarded ADC Award 2023 and Visuelt 2023.

name: Gellix
classification_notes: Displaay class Gellix under sans / grotesque. Mono-linear geometric sans in the #ellix system. Terminals are horizontal or vertical and circle forms connect gracefully onto vertical stems. Sibling to Hellix, Fellix and Yellix.
summary: A geometric mono-linear sans by Martin Vacha in the #ellix family, with horizontal or vertical terminals and gracefully attached circle forms.
description: A geometric sans by Martin Vacha first sketched October 2011 and released August 2015. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, sixteen styles total, plus a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Slant. Gellix sits in the #ellix system, where terminals are horizontal or vertical and circle forms connect gracefully onto stems, contrasted with the diagonal-terminal Hellix and Fellix and the punched Yellix.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Gellix as a mono-linear geometrical sans-serif designed by Martin Vacha around a love for Paul Renner's first Futura sketch. They place it within an #ellix family of four sister cuts, with Gellix carrying horizontal or vertical terminals and circle forms connecting gracefully onto vertical stems, alongside references to Drescher Grotesk, Super Grotesk and Johnston.

name: Greed
classification_notes: Displaay class Greed under sans / grotesque. The Greed Collection has five width families: Condensed, Narrow, Standard, Wide and Extended. Subtle elegant curves and stroke contrast carry the family, italics run at 14 degrees. Originally bespoke for Polansky Gallery, named after a Polanski-directed Vezzoli short.
summary: A five-width high-contrast sans by Martin Vacha, originally drawn for Polansky Gallery, with subtle curves and 14 degree italics.
description: A sans by Martin Vacha first sketched January 2018 and released September 2020. Thirty weights span the collection across five widths, sixty styles total, with a three-axis variable cut on Weight, Slant and Width. Glyphs hold subtle elegant curves and a stroke contrast that lifts the type at display sizes. Italics run at 14 degrees. The collection covers Condensed, Narrow, Standard, Wide and Extended widths.
foundry_blurb: Displaay say Greed was originally created as a bespoke typeface for the Polansky Gallery, commissioned by Anymade Studio, and named after a Roman Polanski-directed 2009 short by Francesco Vezzoli for a fictional fragrance. Designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, the family runs from Condensed to Extended widths with italics angled at 14 degrees.

name: Haffer
subcategory: grotesque system with mono and XH variants
classification_notes: Displaay class Haffer under sans / grotesque. The Haffer Collection holds six families across three designs (Proportional, SemiMono, Mono) and two x-heights (Standard, XH). Sharper, crisper sibling to Matter with strict horizontal and vertical terminals, balanced by Matter-inherited warmth.
summary: A grotesque system by Martin Vacha as the sharper sibling to Matter, with Proportional, SemiMono and Mono designs each in Standard and XH x-heights.
description: A sans by Martin Vacha first sketched October 2018 and released April 2021. Forty-eight weights span six families across three designs (Proportional, SemiMono and Mono) and two x-heights (Standard and XH), ninety-six styles total, with a four-axis variable cut on Weight, Slant, Mono and XHGT. Strict horizontal and vertical terminals give it a precise edge, balanced by warmth carried over from Matter. Latin Extended, Greek and Cyrillic.
foundry_blurb: Displaay frame Haffer as their answer to the search for new interpretations of grotesque sans-serifs, conceived as the sharper, crisper sibling to Matter built around strict horizontal and vertical terminals. Designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, they say its character is distinct enough to be unmistakable, and offer the XH variant for logotypes and headlines wanting an elevated x-height.

name: Hellix
classification_notes: Displaay class Hellix under sans / grotesque. Mono-linear geometric sans, the original ancestor of the #ellix system. Circle forms are punched into vertical stems and terminals are diagonal. Drawn during an internship at the UMPRUM Type Design and Typography Studio.
summary: The original #ellix sans by Martin Vacha, geometric and mono-linear, with circle forms punched into stems and diagonal terminals.
description: A geometric sans by Martin Vacha first sketched October 2011 and released June 2014. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, sixteen styles total, plus a two-axis variable cut on Weight and Slant. As the ancestor of the #ellix system, Hellix punches circle forms into vertical stems and finishes with diagonal terminals, a counterpoint to siblings Fellix, Gellix and Yellix.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Hellix as the original ancestor of their #ellix family, a mono-linear geometrical sans-serif drawn by Martin Vacha during an internship at the Type Design and Typography Studio at UMPRUM in Prague. They cite a love for Paul Renner's first Futura sketch and references to Drescher Grotesk, Super Grotesk and Johnston, with circle forms punched into the vertical stems and diagonal terminals.

name: Jokker
classification_notes: Displaay describes Jokker as a sans-serif grotesque with elevated x-height and diagonal, tight terminals. Glyphs are constructed on geometrically circular forms with reduced thickness at stem connections and otherwise low contrast. Subtle inktraps appear across the family.
summary: A sans-serif grotesque by Martin Vacha with circular forms, tight diagonal terminals and an elevated x-height for compact text settings.
description: A grotesque sans-serif by Martin Vacha released in January 2023, with first sketches dating from October 2018. Seven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, fourteen styles total, plus a variable cut on Slant and Weight axes. Glyphs are built on geometrically circular forms with diagonal, tight terminals, reduced thickness at stem connections and low contrast elsewhere. Short ascenders and descenders compact the line, and inktraps and other small details show up at smaller sizes.
foundry_blurb: Displaay frame Jokker as a grotesque sans-serif designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from Daniel Quisek and Marek Cuban, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite the original spark as a Toblerone wrapper spotted in an airport terminal, where the grotesque R drawn with petite serifs caught their eye, and they describe the design process as a spontaneous, ceramic-clay-like exercise.

name: Lazzer
classification_notes: Displaay describes Lazzer as a sans-serif with narrow proportions, based on Dazzed but with the open terminals closed. The lineage runs through Dazzed, drawn originally as a bespoke face for a cinematic project covering comic, dramatic, technical, action, crime and sci-fi tones.
summary: A narrow sans-serif by Martin Vacha derived from Dazzed, with closed terminals and a cinematic, multi-genre register.
description: A narrow sans-serif by Martin Vacha released in November 2020, derived from Dazzed by closing the open terminals of that family. Seven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, fourteen styles total, plus a variable cut on Slant and Weight axes. Letterforms shift between comic, grotesque, dramatic and quirky moments in a, e, C and G and technical, cold, sci-fi cut-offs in t, f and r. Rounded accent dots sit against squared punctuation for clearer recognition.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Lazzer as a sans-serif with narrow proportions designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from David Rericha and spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They position it as the closed-terminal sibling of Dazzed, originally drawn as a bespoke typeface for a cinematic project intended to cover the full spread of film genres.

name: Matter
classification_notes: Displaay describes Matter as a grotesque with a subtle, warm touch from its lively forms and open terminals. Even the vertical terminals carry a slight angle. The system extends into a variable cut with Weight, x-height, Mono and Slant axes.
summary: A warm grotesque by Martin Vacha drawn from 2015 onward, carrying a four-axis variable system with Weight, x-height, Mono and Slant.
description: A grotesque sans-serif by Martin Vacha first sketched in June 2015 and released in August 2016. The retail family runs to 48 weights and 96 styles, with a variable font on four axes: Weight, x-height, Mono and Slant. Lively forms and open terminals give it a subtly warm character, and the vertical terminals carry a slight angle. The mono axis lets the same skeleton operate as proportional or monospaced, useful for editorial and product systems that share a single voice.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Matter as a grotesque with a subtle, warm touch designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from Marek Cuban, Daniel Quisek and Ilja Bazhanov, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They frame it as a fresh take on familiar grotesque forms, drawn from June 2015 onward and used across many client projects since.

name: Ofform
designer: Marek Suchanek, Kristina Jandova, Martin Vacha
classification_notes: Displaay describes Ofform as built mainly on a 7 by 5 grid, with uppercase and lowercase set to the same height. The design references future, sci-fi, technology and modularity, with thin lines pushing against the stem thickness in some glyphs and diacritics.
summary: A modular display face by Marek Suchanek, Kristina Jandova and Martin Vacha drawn on a 7 by 5 grid, with caps and lowercase at the same height.
description: A modular display face released in March 2020, first sketched in November 2019, designed by Marek Suchanek, Kristina Jandova and Martin Vacha. The typeface is mainly based on a 7 by 5 grid, with uppercases and lowercases sharing the same height. Two styles plus a variable Slant axis cover an upright and a slanted cut. Thin lines run against the heavier stems in some glyphs and diacritics, reinforcing a future, sci-fi, technology and modularity register that suits wordmarks and identity work.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Ofform as a typeface born from a wordmark for fashion brand Ofform 3D, designed by Marek Suchanek, Kristina Jandova and Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Kristina Jandova. They cite the lettering's roots in the idea of folding a stripe of textile fabric, which led to the modular 7 by 5 construction.

name: Panell
classification_notes: Displaay describes Panell as a boxy collection with irregular rounded corners, drawing on a kind of new brutalism inspired by concrete housing blocks. They place it alongside Eurostile (Microgramma), Compacta and Earth while flagging its own identity through an asymmetrically rounded o and shortened arms on f, t and y hidden in stylistic sets.
summary: A boxy geometric sans by Ilya Bazhanov spanning four weights and seven widths, with a brutalist nod to concrete housing blocks and Eurostile.
description: A geometric square sans by Ilya Bazhanov first sketched in October 2021 and released in March 2026. The system runs to 28 weights and 56 styles across four weights from Regular to Bold and seven widths from Compressed to Expanded, with a three-axis variable font covering Weight, Width and Slant. The o is asymmetrically rounded, with one diagonal carrying sharper rounding than the other, which sets the family's rhythm. Stylistic sets hide shortened bottom arms on f, t and y, and the italics lean forward with a sporty energy.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Panell as a punchy take on a familiar answer designed by Ilya Bazhanov with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite Eurostile (Microgramma), Compacta and Earth as reference points while positioning Panell as a futuristic, technical geometric sans-serif drawing on the brutalism of concrete housing blocks.

name: Perfektta
classification_notes: Displaay describes Perfektta as a sans-serif with narrow proportions and clearly visible contrast in the stems. The starting point was a Sardinian road sign with a rhombus-shaped zero, later traced back to the Italian Alfabeto Stretto, a narrowed and bolder modification of British Transport.
summary: A narrow contrast sans by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek, traced from a Sardinian road sign and the Italian Alfabeto Stretto.
description: A narrow contrast sans-serif by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek, first sketched in July 2021 and released in April 2025. Eight weights run from Thin to Black with matching italics, sixteen styles total, plus a variable font on Slant and Weight axes. The construction starts from the rhombus-shaped zero on a Sardinian road sign, with stem contrast pulled through the rest of the alphabet. The witty name marks the contrast between perfect shapes and deliberately imperfect construction.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Perfektta as a narrow sans-serif with visible stem contrast designed by Martin Vacha and Daniel Quisek, with assistance from David Rericha and spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite a photo of a Sardinian road sign with a peculiar zero as the spark, later traced to the Italian Alfabeto Stretto, a narrowed and bolder modification of the original British Transport alphabet.

name: Reckless Collection
classification_notes: Displaay describes Reckless as a serif typeface with a renaissance (old-style) character and a notably raised x-height, drawing from calligraphic influences in early serif designs. The current Reckless Collection replaces the legacy Reckless and Reckless Neue with nine sub-families: three widths (Condensed, Standard, Wide) by three contrasts (S, M, XL).
summary: A nine-family renaissance serif system by Martin Vacha, with three widths and three contrast levels totalling 72 weights and 144 styles.
description: A renaissance old-style serif system by Martin Vacha, first sketched in September 2012 during a UAL London internship in the Monotype archives, released in June 2014 and consolidated as the Reckless Collection in February 2026 (version 3.002). The collection runs to 9 families across three widths (Condensed, Standard, Wide) and three contrasts (S for small text with thick serifs and lower contrast, M for medium sizes, XL for large display). Total range is 72 weights and 144 styles, with a three-axis variable font covering Weight, Contrast and Width. Calligraphic terminals on a, f, j, r, C, c, G, S and s give it its character, with consistent bottoms across a, d and u that resolve the inconsistencies Vacha noticed in Times. Language support covers Latin and Central European plus Vietnamese, Greek and Cyrillic. Note that the legacy Reckless and Reckless Neue have been retired and replaced by this collection.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Reckless as a serif with renaissance (old-style) character and a notably raised x-height, designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from Daniel Quisek and Marek Cuban, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini, and language expansion by Ilja Bazhanov, Irene Vlachou and Tuan Vuong. They cite Bembo's calligraphy terminals, Plantin's a detail, the De Vinne Series specimen book from Barnhart Brothers and Spindler (1907) and Gerrit Dou's Scholar Sharpening His Quill (1635) as direct sources.

name: Roobert
classification_notes: Displaay describes Roobert as a mono-linear, geometric sans-serif with clean horizontal and vertical terminals and smooth stem connections. Originally drawn as a custom typeface for Moogfest 2017, the design echoes the Moog logotype's single-story g with a rounded bottom corner.
summary: A mono-linear geometric sans by Martin Vacha drawn for Moogfest 2017, named after Robert Moog and now covering Latin, Greek, Cyrillic, Arabic, Hebrew, Japanese and Korean.
description: A mono-linear geometric sans by Martin Vacha, first sketched in September 2016 and released in August 2017. The retail family runs to 18 weights and 36 styles across seven weights with matching italics, plus a three-axis variable font on Slant, Weight and Mono. Terminals are clean horizontal and vertical with smooth stem connections, and the bent-pipe motif from the comma is echoed throughout. The single-story g with a rounded bottom corner pulls directly from the Moog logotype. Script coverage is broad: Latin and Central European, revised Greek and Cyrillic, plus Arabic, Hebrew, Japanese and Korean.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Roobert as a mono-linear geometric sans-serif designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from Marek Cuban and Ilja Bazhanov, spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite Moogfest 2017 as the original commission and the Moog logotype as the structural source, with the name a homage to Robert Moog, the American engineer and electronic music pioneer.

name: Saans
classification_notes: Displaay describes Saans as a neutral, untitled, default Swiss-leaning grotesque, originally developed for the foundry's own brand to complement rather than compete with the retail library. It sits as the sans-serif counterpart to Serrif.
summary: A neutral grotesque by Martin Vacha drawn as Displaay's own brand face, deliberately Swiss-leaning and untitled.
description: A neutral grotesque sans by Martin Vacha, first sketched in September 2018 and released in April 2023. The family runs to 18 weights and 36 styles across seven weights with matching italics, plus a three-axis variable font on Weight, Mono and Slant. The brief was internal: a default, untitled face to sit alongside Displaay's retail library without competing with it. The result is a deliberately consistent and moderate Swiss-leaning grotesque. Pairs with Serrif as a sans plus serif default system.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Saans as a typeface developed for the foundry's own internal needs, designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from David Rericha and spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They frame it as a no-style aesthetic typeface in the Swiss tradition, deliberately neutral, universal and untitled to complement rather than compete with their retail catalogue.

name: Season
classification_notes: Displaay describes Season as a system that explores the transformation between sans and serif in continuous stages. The variable font format extends the in-between cuts. The collection covers a unified construction across both sans and serif worlds plus a hybrid stage that blends the two.
summary: A sans-to-serif system by Martin Vacha that morphs between grotesque and antique through a continuous variable axis. Hiiibrand 2024 winner in Professional Typography.
description: A serif and sans hybrid system by Martin Vacha, first sketched in May 2022 and released in October 2024. The family runs to 18 weights and 36 styles across seven weights with matching italics, plus a three-axis variable font on Slant, Weight and SERF (the serif axis). The system shares one construction across sans, hybrid and serif stages, with the variable font letting designers stop anywhere in between. Won the Hiiibrand Awards 2024 in Professional Typography.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Season as an exploration of the transformation from sans to serif designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from David Rericha and spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite the everlasting designer's challenge of pairing sans-serif (grotesque) and serif (antique) typefaces as the brief, and note the family won the Hiiibrand Awards 2024 in Professional Typography.

name: Serrif
classification_notes: Displaay describes Serrif as a default, neutral serif following the construction and principles of Saans, optimised for smaller sizes and longer paragraphs but also working at display. Vertical stress, lower contrast and thicker serifs reference early transitional serifs and later static antiques.
summary: A neutral transitional serif by Martin Vacha built as the serif counterpart to Saans, optimised for text but capable at display.
description: A neutral transitional serif by Martin Vacha, first sketched in February 2022 and released in September 2025. The family runs to 42 weights and 84 styles, with a three-axis variable font on Weight, Width and Slant. Construction follows Saans, with vertical stress, lower contrast and thicker serifs to support readability in long-form text. The aesthetic recalls early transitional serifs and later static antiques. Pairs directly with Saans as a default sans plus serif system.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Serrif as the serif rival to Saans, designed by Martin Vacha with assistance from Ethan Nakache and Marek Cuban and spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They position it as a stand-alone workhorse with the construction and principles of Saans, optimised for smaller sizes and longer paragraphs while still serviceable at display sizes.

name: Teodor
classification_notes: Displaay describes Teodor as a serif with classicist appearance, vertical stress and high contrast, with calligraphic terminals. Standard x-height with smooth stem connections. Cited references include Romana (1892 Schroeder), Caslon 224 (Caslon and Benguiat), Grouch (1970 Carnase and Bonder) and Perpetua (1925 Eric Gill).
summary: A high-contrast classicist serif by Martin Vacha, sibling to Tobias and Reckless, with vertical stress and calligraphic terminals.
description: A classicist serif by Martin Vacha released in November 2019. Eight weights and sixteen styles, with a two-axis variable font on Weight and Slant. Vertical stress, high contrast, standard x-height and smooth stem connections place it firmly in the classicism tradition, while calligraphic terminals soften the formality. Sits alongside Tobias and Reckless as the Vacha serif trio.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Teodor as a serif typeface with classicist appearance designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite Romana (1892 Gustav F. Schroeder), Caslon 224 (1725 William Caslon and Edward Benguiat), Grouch (1970 Tom Carnase and Ronne Bonder) and Perpetua (1925 Eric Gill) as design touchstones, and place Teodor as the brother of Tobias and the sister of Reckless.

name: Tobias
classification_notes: Displaay describes Tobias as a serif with baroque and transitional appearance, with diagonal weight stress as in Old-Style or Renaissance designs. Higher than usual x-height and stem arches that punch into rather than smooth onto the stems. Terminals carry baroque drops and ears, with alternates flipping serif directions.
summary: A baroque transitional serif by Martin Vacha, sibling to Teodor and Reckless, with diagonal stress, punched arches and alternate serif directions.
description: A baroque transitional serif by Martin Vacha released in January 2019. Eight weights and sixteen styles, with a two-axis variable font on Weight and Slant. Diagonal weight stress and stem arches that punch into the verticals carry the baroque DNA, and a higher than usual x-height keeps the family workable in setting. Terminals are built from drops and ears, with alternates that play with different directions of serifs.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Tobias as a serif typeface with baroque and transitional appearance designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite Times and Times New Roman (1904 William Starling Burgess, 1931-1974 Stanley Morison and Victor Lardent), Baskerville (1923 John Baskerville) and Goudy (1908 Frederic W. Goudy) as touchstones, and place Tobias as the brother of Teodor and the sister of Reckless.

name: Wallop
classification_notes: Displaay describes Wallop as a sans-serif family originally commissioned for Wallop magazine, with vertical terminals giving the design sharpness and precision held in tension with rounded forms. The lineage cited includes Berlin West 1942 (Herbert Thannhaeuser), Gill Sans 1928 (Eric Gill) and Johnston 1916 (Edward Johnston).
summary: A humanist sans by Martin Vacha originally drawn for Wallop magazine, with vertical terminals balanced against rounded forms.
description: A humanist sans-serif by Martin Vacha, first sketched in January 2013 and released in May 2019 after the magazine commission. Seven weights run from Thin to Heavy with matching italics, fourteen styles total, plus a variable font on Weight and Slant axes. The family sits in the lineage of Gill Sans, Johnston and Berlin West, with vertical terminals lending precision and rounded forms warming the rhythm. Alternate glyphs for P, R, W, w, m and n widen the typographic palette.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Wallop as a sans-serif type family originally commissioned for Wallop, a magazine dedicated to independent culture, designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite Berlin West 1942 (Herbert Thannhaeuser), Gill Sans 1928 (Eric Gill) and Johnston 1916 (Edward Johnston) as the lineage.

name: Yellix
classification_notes: Displaay places Yellix in the #ellix family alongside Hellix (the original ancestor with circle forms punched into vertical stems and diagonal terminals), Gellix (horizontal or vertical terminals, gracefully connected circles) and Fellix (gracefully connected circles with diagonal terminals). Cited touchstones are Futura (1924-1932 Paul Renner), Drescher Grotesk (2002 Arno Drescher and Nicolai Gogoll), Super Grotesk (1930 Arno Drescher) and Johnston (1915 Edward Johnston).
summary: A geometric sans by Martin Vacha in the #ellix family, drawing on Futura with strict and cold stylistic alternates for a, g, m, n, r and t.
description: A geometric sans-serif by Martin Vacha, first sketched in October 2011 and released in August 2015. Eight weights and sixteen styles, with a two-axis variable font on Weight and Slant. Yellix has horizontal or vertical terminals with circle forms punched into the stems, and stylistic sets that swap in cooler, less geometric alternatives for a, g, m, n, r and t. It sits in the #ellix family alongside Hellix, Gellix and Fellix, each carrying a different combination of terminal cut and circle-to-stem connection.
foundry_blurb: Displaay describe Yellix as a mono-linear geometrical sans-serif designed by Martin Vacha with spacing and kerning by Igino Marini. They cite Paul Renner's first sketch of Futura as the starting affection and place Yellix in the #ellix family with Hellix, Gellix and Fellix, each defined by different terminal and stem-connection treatments. Direct references include Futura (1924-1932 Paul Renner), Drescher Grotesk (2002 Arno Drescher and Nicolai Gogoll), Super Grotesk (1930 Arno Drescher) and Johnston (1915 Edward Johnston).
